---
title: "Why I trust LLMs for code and don't for biology"
description: "Code has a compiler. Biology has the literature. The failure modes diverge."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [llms, biology]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in.]*

When I ask an LLM to write Python, it produces something I can run, test, and reject in seconds. When I ask it to explain a signalling pathway, it produces something that sounds right whether or not it is, and the only check is to go and read the literature myself. Same model, same prompt-quality, very different trust calibrations — because the verification environments are different.

## The asymmetry

*[Code is checkable by the language runtime. A biology claim needs triangulation through papers, often across decades. The LLM's failure modes — confident hallucination of citations, plausible mechanisms that don't exist — are catastrophic in domains where verification is slow.]*

## Where they help in code

*[Boilerplate, mechanical refactors, syntax lookup, idiom translation across languages, regex, test scaffolding. Almost anywhere I'd otherwise read the docs.]*

## Where they help in biology

*[Light literature scoping ("what's the consensus on X"), jargon translation, paper summaries for triage, generating search terms I wouldn't have thought of, drafting figures captions. With sources checked.]*

## Where they fail in biology

*[Hallucinated citations are the obvious one. Worse: plausible-sounding mechanisms that conflate two different pathways, or describe the textbook version of a system known to be more complicated. The model has no signal about which claim is settled and which is contested.]*

## The rule I use

*[LLM output is a first draft of a search query, not a fact. Sources are not negotiable. If I'd cite it in a paper, I've read it.]*
